engineering.translation: I have been systematically beaten down by my own smallness; my judgment regarding the correct course of action has evaporated entirely; consequently I am asking you — not as a colleague-peer, but explicitly as an apprentice — what genuinely constitutes the right move here. Teach me. The verse marks the operational transition from interlocutor to apprentice. The senior engineer, the staff engineer, the engineering manager who has finally articulated the precise sentence 'I do not actually know what to do here; will you teach me?' has crossed the identical threshold that Arjuna crosses. The teaching cannot land before this declaration. The peer-to-peer communication protocol cannot carry instructional signal at all. The operator must explicitly downgrade their own positional rank relative to the teacher for the teacher's actual instruction to become receivable. This is decidedly not humiliation; it is the precise prerequisite for receiving cognitive signal that disagrees with the operator's current frame.
engineering.concrete_scenario: A staff engineer who has been a tech lead for eleven consecutive years walks deliberately into the principal architect's office and closes the door behind them. They open with: 'I've been blocking entirely on the database sharding decision for four full weeks. I've drafted the design document. I've spoken with the vendor representatives. I've benchmarked Postgres logical replication thoroughly against the Vitess proxy approach. I do not know what to recommend, and I no longer trust my own judgment on this particular question — I suspect I'm too emotionally close to the previous design choice. I am not approaching you as a peer-equal here. I am asking you specifically to teach me how you would actually reason through this. śiṣyas te 'haṁ — consider me your student on this question. Tell me how to reason about it correctly.' The principal does not respond by simply handing over the answer. The principal responds the way Krishna responds — by reframing the substrate of the decision (what is invariant under sharding-strategy choice, what is implementation, what is the real unit of value the decision is supposed to serve). The verse names the only request-formulation that unlocks that response. Without it, the principal architect would have merely offered opinions; with it, the principal actually teaches.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if the request for help is a performance — if the operator has already decided what they want and is asking for a teacher's blessing rather than the teacher's instruction. Krishna does not bless Arjuna's preferred option; the chapter overturns Arjuna's frame. If a reader uses 2.7 to extract endorsement of a path they have already chosen, they have not actually surrendered; they have negotiated.
engineering.counter_example: When the operator has not yet done the work that earns the right to ask — when 'teach me' is a substitute for 'I have not invested the effort to learn' — the verse does not apply. Arjuna asks AFTER trying every argument he can muster. Junior engineers who use 2.7 as cover for asking senior engineers to do their thinking have inverted it.
engineering.implication: The exact words you use to ask for help determine which response you can actually receive. Asking as a peer gets you peer-level opinions; asking as a student gets you the teacher's actual reasoning, which is the only thing that can dislodge a stuck frame.
engineering.quotable_line: Until you can say 'I am your student on this question', you will only get peers' opinions, not the teacher's reasoning.

iterations.mutation: v0 generation: BG 2.7 is the pivotal verse of the chapter — formal surrender to the teacher. Engineering layer maps to the staff engineer who explicitly downgrades their position to receive instruction (not opinion) on a stuck architectural decision. Falsifiability rules out negotiation-disguised-as-surrender.
